entry.title: Context-Driven Dashboard Experiences: Role-Based Layouts in SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
What if a single dashboard could completely change depending on who’s viewing it?

While working on a recent project, I explored how scripting can be used to tailor dashboards dynamically based on user roles. Instead of maintaining multiple versions of the same story, I built a single dashboard that adapts in real time – showing only the widgets relevant to each user.

The approach is straightforward: start with a story where all widgets are hidden by default, then use a script to detect the user’s role on load and reveal the appropriate content. This can be as simple as adding a few extra charts, or as powerful as presenting an entirely different dashboard experience.

In this example, I focused on contextualisation by designing views for two distinct audiences – Sales and Marketing – each with their own KPIs.

The API Reference Guide is a great place to start and understand the scripting possibilities: View API Reference

This blog is the first in a series of exploring applied use cases in SAP Analytics Cloud. 

1. Create the Dashboard

I began by building the Sales view, which includes charts, a contextualised dashboard summary, and dynamic name and team text. Once this was in place, I hid all the containers within the page outline except for the dynamic name and team text.

Next, I created the Marketing view, following the same approach – building out the required components and then hiding all the relevant containers. At this stage, when the story is opened, the dashboard appears blank regardless of the user’s role.

Note: I made sure to label the containers (Sales1, Sales2, etc.) so it was easier to find what I wanted to show. 

 

2. Display Content Based on User Role

With the layout complete, I moved on to scripting.

 

Using the on-initialisation script, I first created a variable to capture the user’s team information. I then defined another variable to extract the specific team name. In addition, I created a global variable called “Team” within the page outline, which allows the team name to be displayed dynamically at the top of the dashboard.

 

var teams = Application.getTeamsInfo();
var teamName = teams[0].name;
Team = teams[0].name;

From there, it was simply a matter of using an if statement to control visibility – setting the appropriate elements to visible based on the user’s team. As a result, when a user logs in, they only see the content relevant to them, with the team name updating dynamically. 

if (teamName === "Sales") {
    Sales.setVisible(true);
    Sales1.setVisible(true);
    Sales2.setVisible(true);
    Sales3.setVisible(true);
    Sales4.setVisible(true);
}

if (teamName === "Marketing") {
    Marketing.setVisible(true);
    Marketing1.setVisible(true);
    Marketing2.setVisible(true);
    Marketing3.setVisible(true);
    Marketing4.setVisible(true);
}

 

There are plenty of opportunities for further customisation here. Beyond just showing or hiding charts, you can tailor buttons, apply filters, and even switch branding to create a more personalised experience.

entry.title: Is MCP Necessary? Lessons from AI-Assisted SAP Datasphere Modeling
entry.content:
Abstract: This article explores whether MCP is necessary when automating SAP Datasphere with AI. With SAP's official CLI already available, is building an MCP Server the right approach — or can AI simply call the CLI directly?

Disclaimer: This article represents the author's personal views and practical experience only, and does not represent the position of any company or organization. The technical analysis and cost calculations are based on specific scenarios and are for reference only.

1. Background

1.1 The Scenario

The goal was to use AI to automate SAP Datasphere operations, such as creating tables, views, and analytic models. For more details on this approach, see my previous article: Using Claude Code and Natural Language to Create SAP Datasphere Artifacts.

SAP provides an official command-line tool @sap/datasphere-cli, a fully-featured Node.js CLI that covers the main Datasphere operations:

Main Modules:

  • objects - Manage Datasphere objects (tables, views, analytic models, data flows, etc.) including create, read, update, delete, and deploy
  • spaces - Space management, including creation, configuration, and member management
  • dbusers - Database user management, including user creation, password reset, and permission configuration
  • marketplace - Data marketplace product installation and management
  • tasks - Task chain execution and monitoring
  • cache - Login credential cache management

Command Examples:

# Read the complete JSON structure of an existing object
datasphere objects views read --name EXISTING_VIEW --space MYSPACE

# Create a new object from a JSON file
datasphere objects tables create --file-path table.json --space MYSPACE

# Deploy objects to a specified space
datasphere objects deploy --space MYSPACE

# List database users in a space
datasphere dbusers list --space MYSPACE

# Run a task chain
datasphere tasks chains run --space MYSPACE --name DAILY_ETL

The CLI supports the --output json parameter, outputting structured JSON format, which is ideal for programmatic processing and AI parsing.

1.2 The Question

How do we enable AI (Claude) to call this CLI?

Two mainstream approaches:

  • Approach A: Develop an MCP Server to wrap Datasphere capabilities as MCP tools (via API or CLI) for AI clients
  • Approach B: Use Claude Code Agent to call CLI directly

2. MCP Approach Analysis

2.1 What is MCP

Model Context Protocol (MCP) is a standardized protocol proposed by Anthropic that enables AI to interact with external tools. The MCP Server runs as a separate process and connects AI clients with external services through JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio, enabling standardized AI-tool interaction.

2.2 Existing MCP Implementation

Before deciding to develop my own, I researched existing solutions. There's already an open-source project mariodefe/sap-datasphere-mcp on GitHub that implements an MCP Server for SAP Datasphere. Following the principle of not reinventing the wheel, I analyzed this project to see if it could be used directly.

The project provides 48 MCP tools covering:

Module Tools Operations

Space Management4List spaces, get space info, get space assets, etc.
Data Query6Execute SQL queries, smart queries, relational/analytical queries, etc.
Metadata Browsing12Search tables, get table schema, column distribution analysis, asset catalog browsing, etc.
Database User Management5Create/update/delete users, reset passwords
Task Management4Run task chains, view task logs, task history, etc.
Connection Management3List data source connections, test connections
Data Marketplace2Browse marketplace packages
Other12Tenant info, user info, OAuth scopes, search, etc.

Note: This project primarily calls SAP Datasphere REST API directly (~35 tools), with only the 5 database user management tools invoking the CLI. These APIs are consumption/query-oriented (catalog browsing, metadata exploration, data querying, etc.) and do not support creating tables, views, or analytic models — which is exactly the core requirement described in Section 1.1.

2.3 MCP's Token Consumption Problem

Even setting aside the functional gap, the MCP approach has a cost concern.

The MCP protocol requires returning all tool descriptions via tools/list at initialization. I analyzed the actual data from this project:

  • Total tool description characters: ~62,000 characters
  • Estimated token count: ~15,500 tokens

Problem: These 15,500 tokens are counted in the context at the start of every conversation. Even if the user only wants to perform 1 simple operation, they pay for all 48 tool descriptions — this is a "hidden tax."

Using Claude Sonnet 4.6 as an example ($3/million input tokens), MCP initialization consumes ~15,500 tokens:

Frequency MCP Initialization Cost

Per session$0.047
20 times/day$0.93
Per month$27.90
Per year$339

Note: The above only calculates MCP tool description initialization costs, excluding actual conversation content and output. Pricing data from Anthropic official documentation (April 2026).

3. Alternative: Using Claude Code Agent to Call CLI Directly

3.1 Architecture Comparison

MCP Architecture:

Claude Desktop / Cursor / Other AI Clients
    ↓
JSON-RPC over stdio
    ↓
MCP Server (separate process, 8000+ lines of code)
    ↓
OAuth Handler / API Caller
    ↓
SAP Datasphere API / CLI

Agent Architecture:

Claude Code
    ↓
Bash tool (direct call)
    ↓
SAP Datasphere CLI

3.2 Advantages of the Agent Approach

Comparison MCP (48 tools) Agent

Initialization Tokens~15,5000
Development ComplexityHigh (requires understanding MCP protocol, JSON-RPC, OAuth, etc.)Low (define Skill + call CLI)
Separate ProcessRequiredNot required
Maintenance CostHigh (CLI updates require code changes and release)Low (usually auto-compatible)
Debugging DifficultyHigh (stdio communication)Low (direct Bash output)

3.3 Why CLI is Naturally Suited for AI Agents

CLI commands are inherently atomic operations:

datasphere objects tables create   # Create a table
datasphere objects views read      # Read a view
datasphere objects deploy          # Deploy an object
datasphere dbusers list            # List database users

This fragmented design aligns perfectly with how AI Agents work:

  1. Understand Intent - User says "create a sales analysis model"
  2. Decompose Task - AI breaks it down: create fact table → create dimension tables → create view → create model
  3. Execute Step by Step - Call CLI commands one by one
  4. Adapt Dynamically - Step failed? Read the error message, auto-correct and retry

MCP tries to "package" these atomic operations into predefined composite tools, which actually:

  • Limits AI flexibility
  • Cannot handle unforeseen scenarios
  • Makes it harder to identify which step failed

CLI's fragmentation + AI's reasoning ability = flexible and controllable automation.

4. Other Issues with MCP

4.1 Maintenance Cost After Backend Changes

SAP Datasphere CLI and APIs receive regular updates. With MCP:

CLI or API Update
  → MCP Server code changes
  → Tool description updates
  → inputSchema adjustments
  → Test case updates
  → npm/pypi release new version
  → Users update dependencies

Taking sap-datasphere-mcp as an example, the project currently doesn't implement version detection for either the CLI or the APIs it depends on. This means when SAP releases updates, users may encounter compatibility issues without knowing why.

With Agent directly calling CLI:

  • Claude can directly read datasphere --help to get the latest commands
  • No additional adaptation layer needed
  • Automatically adapts to new features

4.2 MCP Server is Essentially a "Translation Layer"

Claude Request → JSON-RPC → MCP Server → API Call / CLI Command → Parse Response → JSON-RPC → Claude

This translation layer requires:

  1. Parsing Claude's JSON request
  2. Mapping to API endpoints or CLI commands
  3. Executing API calls (OAuth authentication, HTTP requests) or CLI commands
  4. Parsing API responses or CLI output
  5. Reformatting as MCP response

Problems:

  • This translation layer adds complexity and potential errors
  • API changes or CLI output format changes can cause failures
  • Need to handle authentication, error cases, and retries independently

4.3 Limited Flexibility

MCP requires predefined all possible operations. This means:

  • Developers must anticipate all possible user needs in advance
  • Each operation requires defining tool name, description, and parameter structure
  • When new requirements emerge, code must be modified and a new version released

Taking sap-datasphere-mcp's 48 tools as an example, if a user wants to perform an operation that hasn't been predefined — such as creating a Datasphere model (which the project doesn't support), or using a feature added in a new CLI version — they cannot do it through MCP and must wait for the project to update.

Claude Code Agent doesn't have this limitation — it can directly read datasphere --help, understand user intent, dynamically construct commands, and auto-adjust and retry on errors. Whatever capabilities the CLI offers, the AI can use.

5. When Does MCP Make Sense?

5.1 Scenarios Where MCP Fits

Scenario Reason

Services without CLICan only access via REST API
Cross-client ReuseMultiple clients (Claude Desktop, Cursor, etc.) using the same MCP Server
Enterprise StandardizationOrganization needs unified toolsets and permission control
Strict Audit RequirementsNeed to log and audit all AI operations

5.2 Scenarios Where MCP is Not Needed

Scenario Reason

Well-established CLI ExistsDirect calling is simpler and more efficient
Fast IterationSkill modifications take effect immediately, no release process needed
Cost SensitiveMCP initialization tokens are an ongoing hidden cost
Individual/Small TeamNo need for cross-client reuse

6. Conclusion

6.1 For the SAP Datasphere Scenario

Based on my practice, with a well-established CLI available, I chose Claude Code Agent over MCP. Key considerations:

  • SAP provides a fully-featured CLI (@sap/datasphere-cli)
  • CLI supports JSON output, AI can parse directly
  • Agent approach is more flexible, can automatically adapt to CLI updates
  • Avoids MCP initialization token consumption

But this is just my choice based on specific requirements, and may not apply to all scenarios.

6.2 Open Question

While writing this article, I've been thinking about a question: As AI capabilities continue to grow, what is the long-term value of "middleware" like MCP?

When AI can already understand CLI documentation, parse JSON output, and auto-adjust based on errors, do we still need a predefined tool layer? Will MCP's value increase as AI capabilities improve, or will it gradually be replaced by more flexible Agent approaches?

Perhaps time will tell. Feel free to share your perspective!

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere News in March
entry.content:
SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere News in March

*** UPDATE: Added the March Feature Highlight Video ***

The minor news is that I was on vacation 🏝️ hence the late news blog for March 😉
but my colleagues did not stop delivering releases while I was out 🚀

Plenty of small but super useful features and enhancements being delivered with our two releases in March 🔥

My monthly top features video for March 2026 is a little late this time due to my vacation, but will follow on YouTube 📺 the next days on the usual
🔗  Top Features Playlist: https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PL3ZRUb1AKkpS8bwmAREXo78O2xccXzoiy
🔗  March Feature highlights: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IoFg1SadBY8

 

 

My top features in March

 

Currency Conversion

There are two updates with currency conversion:

  1. Shift for Currency Conversion
    In the analytic model, you can now control whether currency conversion applies a shift of the decimal points or not.
  2. Simplified Sharing of Views Containing Currency Conversion
    You can now simply share SQL views containing a currency conversion column to another space, without needing also to share all the supporting currency conversion entities too. Very convenient.

➡️  More information: Create a Currency Conversion Column in a Graphical View and Enabling Currency Conversion with TCUR* Tables and Views.

 

DAC Support for associated dimensions of fact data

When creating an association from a fact to a dimension, you can now choose to apply the dimension's data access controls directly to the fact data.

You can see any data access controls applied in this way in a subsection Applied via Dimensions under the list of Data Access Controls in the side panel.

➡️  More information.

 

Support Large Parquet Files for Object Store Targets

You can now enable to create large files in Replication Flows when replicating data to supported cloud object store targets using the Parquet file format. This creates fewer and larger files during the initial load.

➡️  More information.

 

Manage Replication Flow Runs via the Command Line

You can now manage Replication Flow runs via the Command Line interface (CLI), meaning you can run, stop, and obtain the status of replication flows via the command line.

➡️  More information.

 

Email Notification for Expiring Consent

You will now automatically receive an email notification before your authorization consent expires. This will help ensure your scheduled tasks will continue without interruption.

➡️  More information.

 

Define Z-ORDER COLUMNS While Running an Optimize Task

While running an optimize task for your local table (file), you can now define Z-order columns to co-locate the data by columns and therefore reduce the amount of data that needs to be read.

Z Ordering co-locates similar data in the same files, which allows for better file skipping and faster queries. The difference from partitioning is that Partitioning creates physical folders, which is good for low-cardinality data like a region, Boolean values or the likes. Z-Ordering optimizes the file layout within those folders based on high-cardinality data customer Ids, time stamps and the likes.

➡️  More information.

 

Data Lineage enhancement for the BW DTPs

A new link type (dashed line) has been added to the Impact and Lineage Analysis diagram for Data Transfer Processes (DTP) and their associated Transformations (TRFN) across all BW systems. You can gain complete data traceability control by visualizing how one or more transformations are connected to and used within DTPs, enabling better understanding of data flow and dependencies in your BW environment.

➡️  More information.

 

 

Webinar Series: The Next Era of Business Data

One session remaining on the great sessions of this series. In case you could not make it live, all sessions are recorded and I added the links to the recordings below.

In case you are interested to join the last session live, please feel free to
➡️ sign up now: SAP BDC: The Next Era of Business Data

🔹Jan 29 | Discover SAP Business Data Cloud in 2026 - recording

🔹Feb 05 | The Smart Path to SAP BW Modernization - recording

🔹Feb 19 | Empowering SAP Datasphere Users with SAP Business Data Cloud Innovations - recording

🔹Feb 26 | From Data to Decisions: Analytics with SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP BDC - recording

🔹Mar 12 | From Data Silos to Business Insight: The Power of BDC Connect - recording

🔹Mar 19 | An Architect's Blueprint for SAP Business Data Cloud as the Foundation for Enterprise AI - recording

🔹Mar 26 | Design. Govern. Deliver. Data Products - Data Product Studio - recording

🔹Apr 16 | Breaking Down the SAP BDC Pricing Model

 

 

More blogs about SAP BDC and Datasphere to check out 👇

  • BDC : BW Modernization - Key to Innovation for SAP Customers

entry.title: I tricked Datasphere into running my custom scaler functions
entry.content:
Introduction - Welcome, once again. 

Hello, and welcome to an easter edition of my hit-series of blogposts, where I try to help my fellow developers get to grips with SAP Datasphere .

Easter is over, but today I've put on my Reverse-Easter-Bunny costume, and have dug up a few golden easter eggs for you. 
Even if you think the word "Scalar Functions" sounds like scary techno-babble, I still think there is something of values in here, that you might find useful.

And do not worry, if you are hoping for my usual sprinkling of sarcasm or was hoping I would make fun of SAP and their interesting design decisions, you will not be disappointed. My God, are we going to cry. I mean laugh. Laugh. Yes. 

Today we will be going through the following:

  1. What is a Database User Group, and why you should learn to love it.
  2. How to re-enable SYS.DUMMY functionality (because shouldn't we?)
  3. How to build and implement custom scalar functions across your Datasphere solution
  4. Jail-break the Graphical View editor, so we can gas-light it into doing its f#!&ing job.

Happy reading 🙂 

1: Why you should always create a "Database User Group" 

So, if you have ever been in an hour-long townhall meeting, you might have mindlessly scrolled through the Datasphere documentation (Only me? Alright then), in hope of stumbling over something good. 

On one such occasion, I came across how to create a database user group, and thought myself: 

"What the hell is that? Why should I care?"

In the documentation, we get the following:

"Users with an administrator role can create database user groups in SAP Datasphere to allow users to work in a sandboxed area in the underlying SAP HANA Cloud database, unattached to any space. These users can transfer an existing data warehouse implementation into the SAP Datasphere database or do any other work in SAP HANA Cloud and then make it available to one or more spaces as appropriate"

Which is a pretty boring few sentences, but here is the key value: You can create database objects, that can be accessed across your solution. 
This allows you to use the full suite (almost, let's not get ahead of ourselves) of HANA SQL functionality, which can offer a lot more programmability than Datasphere in general. 

1.1: Setup your Database User Group  

You can follow the documentation to create the "Admin" user for your group. 
Open the "Database Explorer", provide the password, and open the SQL console.

Note: I've used CHR_GRA as the namespace in my examples, you can use whatever fits you.

In your SQL console, you will now have to:

  • Create a non-admin user
  • Create a schema 
-- Step 1: Create User, Schema and role.
CREATE USER DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>#<USER> PASSWORD Password123 SET USERGROUP DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>;
CALL "DWC_GLOBAL"."CREATE_USERGROUP_SCHEMA"
(
	SCHEMA_NAME => 'DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>#<SCHEMA_NAME>',
	OWNER_NAME => 'DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>#<USER>'
					);

Technical side-note: By default, HANA creates a unique schema for each user. So it's good practise to create a separate schema, that is independent of any user. 

1.2: Switch user

In the left side of the Database Explorer, right-click on your database and select "Add Database with Different User":

Use the user ID and password you created above. 

Open a new SQL console, and validate that you are now connected to your user:

1.3: Cool... Now what?

Now you can do whatever you want. 
You can create custom tables, views, functions and procedures in the schema you set up and expose it to the spaces in your solutions. 

2: Re-enabling SYS.DUMMY

So, if you've ever read any of the documentation for HANA SQL, you'll see endless references to a table called DUMMY. 

An example could be getting today's date

SELECT current_date
FROM sys.dummy;

Which may seem odd, but the (somewhat simple) explanation is that HANA will not execute a query without a source. 
So, the table SYS.DUMMY only ever contains one row, so you can use it as a workaround, for cases where you don't want to query data, but needs to execute something else. 

In SAP Datasphere , the above query will fail, if you try it in an SQL view:

But hell, sometimes you just want a query without a source. So, how can we re-establish it? 

In our database your schema, we can create a dummy view, and expose it in our space:

SET SCHEMA DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>#<SCHEMA_NAME>; 

CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW "DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>#<SCHEMA_NAME>"."DUMMY" AS SELECT "DUMMY" from "SYS"."DUMMY";

Which creates a wrapper view. 
Why is that required? Because your spaces will always try to execute an SQL query inside it's own schema, and will not be able to find the SYS schema. 

When the view is created, you'll have to grant access to your space by running the following SQL:

CALL "DWC_GLOBAL"."GRANT_PRIVILEGE_TO_SPACE" (
	OPERATION => 'GRANT', 
	PRIVILEGE => 'SELECT', 
	SCHEMA_NAME => 'DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>#<SCHEMA_NAME>', 
	OBJECT_NAME => '', 
SPACE_ID => '<YOUR_SPACE>');

 In your Datasphere space, you can now deploy the view, and you're back in action!

"But Christian, can't I just create a local table in my space and do the exact same thing?"

Well yes. But I think this served as a nice example of how you can use your DB user group to access system information, that is not otherwise available to you. 

3: Implementing custom scalar functions 

Alrighty, so on to the real magic. 

If you are not familiar with SQL development, a bit of background might be useful. 

In SQL in general, and HANA SQL as well, you can build User-Defined Functions, that allow you to implement more complex SQL logic, such as control structures (loops) and logical operators, and execute dynamic SQL. HANA offers two kinds of User-Defined Functions that you can build.

  1. Table function: Which returns a full view, that you can call from a SELECT statement. 
  2. Scalar function: Which are functions you can call within a view, and returns a single value for each row.
    When you create a "Calculated column" in your views, you are using a scalar function. Functions such as IFNULL, COALESCE, LOWER and so on are all scalar functions, that you can use in your views. 

The value of scaler functions is that they allow you to create re-usable custom logic, that can be utilized across your entire solution. 
In this post, I'll create two functions as a few examples, which should give you an idea of how to utilize scalar functions in your solution. 

3.1: Example 1 - Harmonization of amount columns 

One challenge we always encounter in a data warehouse is the standardization of data formats when combining data from several sources. An example that may be very common now with the introduction of Seamless Planning, is combining planning data with actuals data from an ERP system. 

By default, measures from SAC will be decimals with 7 decimals; whereas actuals from wither BW or SAP will be a decimal with 2 decimals. When combining the numbers in Datasphere, it will do it's best, but it may lead to either a loss of precision, or an ungodly amount of decimals in your reports. 

So, what if you want to enforce a consistent data format across your solution? Well, you could use a scalar function. 

In this example, I have decided I want all my amounts to be a Decimal(17, 2). Why? Because I grew up with BW, and 17,2 just feels like the objectively correct format. You may disagree (and you would be objectively wrong) and choose another format. 

So, open your Database Explorer, and open an SQL console, and make sure it's connected to the user your created in step 1.1. 

First, set the correct schema:

SET SCHEMA DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>#<YOUR_SCHEMA>;

Then you can create your function as below:

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION clean_amount_column (
	in_amount DECIMAL
)
returns out_amount decimal(17,2) -- Defines return type
LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
SQL SECURITY DEFINER -- Definer means it will use your DB user's rights, not whoever is using your report.
READS SQL DATA -- Makes sure the function is READ-ONLY. Functions almost always are, but it will help the compiler optimize function calls.
DETERMINISTIC -- Tells the compiler that the function will always return the same output, that means the results are cached for the runtime session, and can improve performance.

as 
BEGIN


DECLARE v_num DECIMAL(34,10);

    -- Null handling: If NULL, return 0. 
    IF in_amount IS NULL THEN
        out_amount := 0;
    ELSE 
		-- Convert to highest possible precision. Ensure we can handle scientific magnitude numbers. 
	    v_num := TO_DECIMAL(:in_amount, 34, 10);
		v_num := ROUND(v_num,2); -- Round to two decimals.
	    out_amount := TO_DECIMAL(:v_num, 17, 2); -- Convert to final DECIMAL(17,2)
    END IF;

END;

A few notes on the script:

  • CREATE OR REPLACE: If a function with that name, in that schema already exists, it will be overwritten. You may not want to accidently overwrite existing objects. In that case, you can use CREATE. 
  • SQL SECURITY DEFINER: This means that the function will run with the rights of your DB user, rather than the user calling. This may not be what you want, so change to Invoker if you need that instead.
  • DETERMINISTIC: Is something you can use if you can promise the server that your function will always return the same result, given the same input, which improves performance, because results can be cached. Functions that require NOW() or TODAY() or CURRENT_DATE can not be deterministic. 

When you have run the script, you should be able to find your function in your DB Schema:

You can right-click and select "Generate SELECT Statement", and test your function. 

SELECT 12.747456 as Input, '12.76' as ExptectedOutput, "DWCDBGROUP#CHR_GRA#REPORTING"."CLEAN_AMOUNT_COLUMN"(12.764456).OUT_AMOUNT AS OUT_AMOUNT FROM DUMMY
UNION
SELECT 124545678.767456 as Input, 124545678.77 as ExptectedOutput, "DWCDBGROUP#CHR_GRA#REPORTING"."CLEAN_AMOUNT_COLUMN"(124545678.767456).OUT_AMOUNT AS OUT_AMOUNT FROM DUMMY
UNION
SELECT NULL as Input, 0.00 as ExptectedOutput, "DWCDBGROUP#CHR_GRA#REPORTING"."CLEAN_AMOUNT_COLUMN"(NULL).OUT_AMOUNT AS OUT_AMOUNT FROM DUMMY
UNION
SELECT '123.678' as Input, '123.68' as ExptectedOutput, "DWCDBGROUP#CHR_GRA#REPORTING"."CLEAN_AMOUNT_COLUMN"('123.678').OUT_AMOUNT AS OUT_AMOUNT FROM DUMMY

I've tested with a few values, including NULL and even a string 

INPUTEXPTECTEDOUTPUTOUT_AMOUNT

NULL

00.00
123.678123.68123.68
12.74745612.7612.76
124545678.767456124545678.77124545678.77

When you are happy with your function, you need to provide access to your spaces. To do this you will need to:

  • Create a public synonym for your function
  • Grant EXECUTE rights to your space to functions in your schema. 

The following statement may not make sense, but the technical reasons why this works is way beyond the scope of this post. So you'll just have to trust me. 
If you want the technical breakdown; let me know, and I might include an appendix to this post at some point. 

-- Create a public synonym - DO NOT REPLACE any existing synonyms.
-- If you're a puritan, you can always add a Z to your function and / or synonym. 
CREATE PUBLIC SYNONYM ZCLEAN_AMOUNT for DWCDBGROUP#CHR_GRA#REPORTING.clean_amount_column;

The public synonym is the name your function will have in your views, so give it a name good name. 

Get the ID of the space you want to give access to. Then run the following commands in an SQL console:

GRANT SELECT, EXECUTE ON SCHEMA "DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>#<YOUR_SCHEMA>" TO "<SPACE_ID>"."<SPACE_ID>::default" WITH GRANT OPTION;
GRANT SELECT, EXECUTE ON SCHEMA "DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>#<YOUR_SCHEMA>" TO <SPACE_ID> WITH GRANT OPTION;

Note: the format is important. The quotes ( " ) must be there, otherwise you'll get errors. 

So, how do you implement it?

Back in Datasphere, go to the space you granted access to, and create an SQL view. 
You can now call your function by it's synonym, and feed it your inputs:

Important note: Datasphere, however, will not correctly derive the return type of your custom scalar functions, but will inherit  the datatype of your input. 
In my case, "Lines" is an integer, and the hard-coded values is a double, so I had to go in and manually change the format. 

"But Christian, if I have to manually adjust the data type anyway, what is the value here?"

Well, yeah, that is a pain. However, even so, I would still argue that things like consistent null-behaviour, string-conversions etc., still have value. 
Also, please remember that Datasphere dosen't know it actually supports customer scalar functions. This is basically me gaslighting it into running my functions, so a few quirks are to be expected. 

3.2: Example 2: Consistent Date-conversions

Another colossal pain in the backend in any data modelling scenario is how to manage dates.
One absolute banger of timewaster is to get the CALMONTH (YYYYMM) from a date. Year, month, week, etc. all have standard functions implemented, but CALMONTH does not. 
The formula is pretty simple, but so help me God, do I always manage to get it wrong, for some reason. (I am fully prepared to accept that the reason may be that I'm an idiot, but there is no reason to personal).

So, for this example, I'll show you another happy little easter egg: A scaler function can return several values, and can act like a class with different methods. 

In this example, we'll take a basic SAP datestring (YYYYMMDD) and provide a single function, that can return year, month, calmonth, calweek, halfyear and quarter. 
You can append it as you prefer. 

SET SCHEMA DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_SCHEMA>#<YOUR_SCHEMA>; 

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION transform_sap_date (
	in_datestring nvarchar(8)
)
returns 
	out_year nvarchar(4), --YYYY
	out_month nvarchar(2), -- MM
	out_calmonth nvarchar(6), --YYYYMM
	out_calweek nvarchar(6), -- YYYYWW
	out_quarter nvarchar(1), -- Q
	out_halfyear nvarchar(1) --H
LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
SQL SECURITY DEFINER 
READS SQL DATA 
DETERMINISTIC 

as 
BEGIN

DECLARE lv_sql_date date;

    -- Null handling: If NULL, null. 
    IF in_datestring IS NULL or length(in_datestring) != 8 THEN
		out_year := null;
		out_month := null;
		out_calmonth := null;
		out_calweek := null;
		out_quarter := null;
		out_halfyear := null;
    ELSE 
    	lv_sql_date := to_date(:in_datestring);
		out_year := to_nvarchar(year(:lv_sql_date));
		out_month := lpad(month(:lv_sql_date),2,'0');
		out_calmonth := concat(:out_year, :out_month);
		out_calweek := concat(:out_year, lpad(week(:lv_sql_date),2,'0'));
		out_quarter := left(ROUND( month(:lv_sql_date) / 3, 0, ROUND_UP),1);
		IF month(:lv_sql_date) < 6 then
			out_halfyear :=  '1';
		else
			out_halfyear :=  '2' ;
		end if;
		
    END IF;

END;

-- Create a public synonym - DO NOT REPLACE any existing synonyms.
-- If you're a puritan, you can always add a Z to your function and / or synonym. 
CREATE PUBLIC SYNONYM ZDATE for DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_SCHEMA>#<YOUR_SCHEMA>.transform_sap_date;

You can now test your function. You can get each output by specifying it with a .<IDENTIFYER> in each column:

SELECT 
	'20260408' as InputDate,
	"ZDATE"('20260408').OUT_YEAR AS OUT_YEAR,
	"ZDATE"('20260408').OUT_MONTH AS OUT_MONTH,
	"ZDATE"('20260408').OUT_CALMONTH AS OUT_CALMONTH,
	"ZDATE"('20260408').OUT_CALWEEK AS OUT_CALWEEK,
	"ZDATE"('20260408').OUT_QUARTER AS OUT_QUARTER,
	"ZDATE"('20260408').OUT_HALFYEAR AS OUT_HALFYEAR
FROM DUMMY

So, .OUT_YEAR gives you the year, and so on. Which gives you the following result:

Which is a great success! 

So, in your view, you can now pass a date or a datestring to a function, and get whatever you need:

Note: Again, DSP gets confused about the datatype, and wants to give me a string(100):

So you will have to change that, but you can't have it all. 

3.3: A few notes

Alrighty! So now we've seen how to create and implement custom, reusable logic across your Datasphere solution. 

But it is not without headaches. 
First and foremost, SQL artifacts can't be included in the transport packages, so you will have to repeat the process for each system in your landscape. 

So, when transporting, the sequence is: (1) Make sure all the spaces are created, (2) create your DB group, schema and user, (3) Create or replace all the SQL objects, (4) Import your Datasphere views and models.

4: Jailbreaking the Graphical Editor

So, you may have noticed that up until now I've created SQL views to test my functions. 
The reason is quite simple: The Graphical editor has access to fewer functions than the SQL editor.

However, that is easily fixed, if you are willing to fiddle with the debug console a bit. 

Note: This works now, but technically it is a bit on the naughty side, so SAP may patch it out in the future. 
However, it they patch it with the same speed that the add new features, I'm guessing this should make it to at least 2027Q3 before they notice. 

So, the "allowed functions" in the graphical viewer is loaded once as a static list, the first time the editor is opened. 

When you "Validate" and "Deploy" your view, the functions are validated against the static list, before it is deployed. If a function is not in that list, the view will not deploy. 

So, the obvious fix is to smack in a breakpoint when the list is loaded, manually add your functions, and then deploy the view as always. 

Note: This process must be repeated every time a view is opened, and must be done when the editor initially loads. 

So, here is what you do. 

  1. Open the view you want to change, or click "New Graphical View"
  2. Once the view has loaded open the development console for your browser (either press F12, or right-click -> 'Inspect') 
  3. Go to 'Sources' and right-click the folder with your tenant name and 'Search in all files':

    Then, search for the following: FUNCTION_NAME_TO_FUNCTION. This might take a minute or two. You'll get results that look something like this:

    In the file with the most hits, click any to open up the chunk. 

  4. In the open chunk, locate a function that contains "class a", and place a breakpoint AFTER the end of that function:
  5. Refresh the page, but do not close the debugger.
  6. When the debugger hits your breakpoint, find the console. 
  7. Locate your black trench-coat, 90s hacker glasses. Take a deep breath.
  8. Mandatory step. Say "I've hacked the mainframe" out loud. You may also whisper, "I'm in", if you are currently in an open office setup. 
  9. In the console, you can type a.ALL to see an array of all the functions:

    You can now see all the info required for a function to be included. 

  10. (Optional step) Open one of the functions and inspect the elements. Please take special note that the documentation in a RAW-string HTML document. 

    If you feel like it, you can either roll your eyes, do a facepalm or a similar display of "what the actual **bleep**". 

  11. From here, you can now push your functions to the class, and they will be legal in your graphical view. 
a.ALL.push({Function: 'ZCLEAN_AMOUNT', InTypes: 'decimal', Inputs: 1, Return: 'decimal', definition: 'amount Converter (UDF)', items: [{"key": ""}], prepared: true})​

Then, you also have to add the function to the FUNCTION_NAME_TO_FUNCTION dictionary:

a.FUNCTION_NAME_TO_FUNCTION['ZCLEAN_AMOUNT'] = {Function: 'ZCLEAN_AMOUNT', InTypes: 'decimal', Inputs: 1, Return: 'decimal', definition: 'amount converter (UDF)', items: [{"key": ""}], prepared: true}​

I'm not sure why, but I'm sure there are good, complex, technical reasons why (🙃).

  • Resume the script (F8) and let the view load finish:
  • You can now close the debugger. 
  • Your function is now legal, you can use it, deploy your view, and everything will compile and run without issue. 
    And why shouldn't it? SQL views and Graphical views both compile to the same HANA server. 
    The only reason graphical views behave differently is because SAP has actively restricted it. The only trick is to trick the graphical editor to not check. 

    Note: In all fairness, the Graphical view has to translate a graph to an SQL query, so there has to be some logic / special handling. Buuuuuut the list has remained static for over a year, so at this point it is just negligence.

So, that's it! 
And please note you can add any number of functions while the debugger is open. 
Both your own, or any function from the function reference that you need. 
Want to use JSON_QUERY functions for JSON extractions? No problem. Add it in, deploy, enjoy. 

Note: Be careful to add window functions, or aggregate functions, that are not supported. The view may compile and deploy, but it may behave oddly. 

Unfortunately, you'll have to repeat this process every time you open a graphical view.
Opening a view that contains a jail-break function, without adding it to the legal list, will result in an error, and prevent you from deploying. 

4: Conclusion

So, congratulations for making it all the way through that. I've tried my best to hit a balance between technical details, without it becoming overwhelming. 

If you've any questions or if anything was unclear, please leave a comment and I'll either reply or update the post, to improve clarity. 

So, by now you've learned :

  1. How to access the HANA Cloud tenant, and create custom SQL objects to implement reusable code. 
  2. Allow your spaces to consume said objects in your modelling
  3. Jail-break the graphical view editor, to include missing functions, without waiting 6 years for a feature request. 

Happy reading, happy coding and I hope you had a wonderful easter.

entry.title: Season 10 of Let's Talk Data Podcast Series: A focus on the data professionals that make it happen
entry.content:
Season 10 of SAP Let’s Talk Data is built for practitioners who make data work. We’re shifting from high-level storytelling to hard-earned, technical lessons from engineers, scientists, stewards, and architects who design, operate, and scale real-world data systems. Expect deep, operational conversations about the challenges, trade-offs, and best practices that turn data into repeatable, observable deliverables.

 What we’ll dig into

We will cover topics like your data platform architecture, data engineering at scale, (master) data quality and observability, data modelling from data practitioners and partners.

  • Governance and security: privacy-preserving analytics, metadata management, access policy enforcement, cataloging, encryption, and auditability in regulated environments.
  • Organizational and delivery practices: cross-functional workflows between platform, product, and analytics teams, prioritization under competing SLAs, and techniques for reducing friction between ops and research.

Here is a look at the first episodes

  • A Day in the Life of a Data Steward — Behind the Scenes of Trusted Data

What does a day in the life of a data steward really look like? This podcast pulls back the curtain on the people who keep enterprise data trustworthy, usable, and compliant. We will discuss how these data steward navigate through the rhythms of their day—triaging data incidents, curating metadata, negotiating business definitions, enforcing access controls, and coordinating fixes across engineering, analytics, and business teams. We explore both process realities and the best practices in place to ensure trust.

  • All in on AI: SAP BDC and SAP Business AI - coming soon

Leona Hasani is an SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) expert who specializes in all things data analysis and data management for BDC. In this podcast, she will dive deep into how BDC can help a data scientist leverage all the BDC features and technologies to maximize the value. If you are a data scientist, work with one, or in general, manage some form of the data environment in your team, then this podcast will help you understand all the tools available to you when using SAP Business Data Cloud.

Join us!

If you build data pipelines, ship analytics products, steward sensitive data, or operate ML systems, this season is engineered to deliver actionable guidance you can adapt to your stack and scale.

Subscribe to follow Season 10 (on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, YouTube, and more) and continue the conversation with the Data Professionals Community

entry.title: Welcome to Season 10 of SAP Let’s Talk Data!
entry.content:
Season 10 of SAP Let’s Talk Data is built for practitioners who make data work. We’re shifting from high-level storytelling to hard-earned, technical lessons from engineers, scientists, stewards, and architects who design, operate, and scale real-world data systems.

This season we’re putting the spotlight on the people who make data work: the data professionals shaping how businesses run. From data engineers and scientists to stewards, analysts, and architects, these roles don’t operate in isolation. Together they balance trust, governance, scale, and speed to turn data into real business impact.

Season 10 is about how ideas become impact. Expect candid conversations with practitioners who lead data operations, build analytics products, steward sensitive information, and translate insights into action. We’ll highlight real challenges—conflicting priorities, organizational friction, and technical trade-offs—and lessons learned from teams that turned those challenges into advantages. Whether you’re building data models, owning data quality, or shaping strategy, these episodes are designed to give you usable perspectives, actionable tips, and a clearer view of how data professionals connect to the broader business mission.

Because data professionals aren’t just supporting the business, they’re driving it forward.

Here is a look at the first episodes

  • A Day in the Life of a Data Steward — Behind the Scenes of Trusted Data

What does a day in the life of a data steward really look like? This podcast pulls back the curtain on the people who keep enterprise data trustworthy, usable, and compliant. We will discuss how these data steward navigate through the rhythms of their day—triaging data incidents, curating metadata, negotiating business definitions, enforcing access controls, and coordinating fixes across engineering, analytics, and business teams. We explore both process realities and the best practices in place to ensure trust.

  • All in on AI: SAP BDC and SAP Business AI - coming soon

Leona Hasani is an SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) expert who specializes in all things data analysis and data management for BDC. In this podcast, she will dive deep into how BDC can help a data scientist leverage all the BDC features and technologies to maximize the value. If you are a data scientist, work with one, or in general, manage some form of the data environment in your team, then this podcast will help you understand all the tools available to you when using SAP Business Data Cloud.

Join us!

Subscribe to follow the season and join the conversation as we lift the curtain on the people and practices powering data-driven organizations.

  • Spotify
  • Apple Podcasts
  • YouTube
  • And more…

What further connection for collaboration and growth?

Join the conversation with the Data Professionals Community!

The group welcomes perspectives across all data and AI topics. These types of projects deliver their greatest value when a team of colleagues work together towards the final solution. Thus, the spirit of this community.

entry.title: From Raw Data to Data Products: End-to-End Implementation using SAP Business Data Cloud
entry.content:
This is my first blog and I will walks through a practical implementation of how data from SAP S/4HANA can be ingested into the Object Store, transformed into a business-ready Data Product within SAP Datasphere, and securely shared via the BDC Cockpit to Databricks using delta-enabled data sharing. Along the way, we will cover the prerequisites, step-by-step setup, and key considerations to successfully build and share a Delta Share Data Product in SAP Business Data Cloud.

Architecture illustrates how data flows from SAP S/4HANA into the Object Store, is transformed into a Data Product within SAP Datasphere, and is then shared via BDC Cockpit to Databricks using delta-enabled data sharing.

Pre-requisite:

  1. If your SAP Datasphere tenant is part of an SAP Business Data Cloud formation, then you can create data products for SAP Business Data Cloud.
  2. A Data Provider Profile should be created.
  3. An Object Store Space should be created and the required roles should be assigned.

Object Store Space

The Object Store space has been created and deployed in datasphere, and the required roles have been assigned to create replication flows.

Replication Flow

Create a replication flow for the I_PRODUCT table in the Object Store space, with the table load type set to Initial and Delta.

Delta Table

This is generated after the successful execution of the replication flow.

Data Provider Profile

To create a Delta Share data product, you must create a data provider profile with a dedicated visibility option called Formations.

  1. From the Data Sharing Cockpit, go to the My Data Provider Profile app, click Create Data Provider Profile to open the data provider page.
  2. Fill out your profile.
  3. Select the visibility option Formations.
  4. Enter a Namespace. A prefix based on your landscape's name is automatically generated to ensure consistency.
  5. Once you've created your Formations profile, you can create a Delta Share data product.

Creating a Delta Share Data Product

  1. In Data Sharing Cockpit > My Data Products, click Create Data Product.
  2. Enter the Business Name and the mandatory Technical Name of the data product.
  3. Fill out the Product Details.

As you're creating a data product as a Formations provider, the matching context is automatically assigned by default. 

Similarly, the Data Shipment is automatically set by default to Integrated Delivery, and the Delivery Mode to Delta Share. You cannot change these values.

  1. In the Pricing section, the Contract Type is also set by default to Free, and you cannot change it either.

Create Data Product
Select the Object Store Space.

 Context in new Data product will display the data provider name.

Shipment types: Integrated Delivery

The data is copied directly into the space that the consumers select.

Add Product Artifacts
Select the space where your local tables containing the data are included. The Artifact Space is automatically set to SAP HANA DATA LAKE storage type instead of regular HANA Cloud spaces.

Note: The SAP HANA DATA LAKE spaces don't contain any views so you can only add local tables and Only tables with supported column data types are displayed.

The lifecycle state of the new data product is set to Draft. To make it available for consumers, select the data product from the page My Data Products.

Click Switch Status to change the status to "Listed".

Status of the Data Product will be updated to Listed

Catalog users will be able to search for and evaluate these data products.

  • When the catalog is accessed from an SAP Datasphere system, users can install the data product to a space.
  • When the catalog is accessed from an SAP Business Data Cloud system, users can share the data product to a target system.

If at a later time you change the lifecycle status of a data product to Delisted, the data product will not be available in the catalog anymore. If the data product is installed to a space or shared to a target system, users won't be able to uninstall the data product or remove the shares until its status is changed back to Listed. 

Note: You can delete Delta Share data products once they are uninstalled from all SAP Datasphere spaces and are no longer shared with any tenants.

When you delete a Delta Share data product, the system also deletes the corresponding data share tables from SAP HANA DATA LAKE spaces.

Please note that once you've deleted a data product, you cannot create a new one with the same technical name.

SAP Business Data Cloud
Go to BDC Cockpit and search for data product.

Make sure you can share a data product by checking the following statuses:
The data product's release status is Active, its lifecycle status is Active, and its functional status is Current.

Share Data Product

Context:

Please remember that data products can only be shared to different systems within the same formation. You're not allowed to share them to the same system they were created from. If the target system you want to select is the same system that created the data product, use that system's native sharing capabilities instead. 

  1. In the side navigation area, choose (Catalog & Marketplace) > (Search).
  2. On the catalog search page, use the filters or the search to find the data product you want and choose it to open its details page.

In the dialog, choose Add Target to share the data product.

Choose the system you want to share to. If only one target is available, it's automatically selected. Here select Databricks system

Enter a share name
The name that you enter is the name that users will see in the SAP Databricks, and it cannot be changed after the data product is shared. Select the default workspace. You can select to share the data product with up to five workspaces, including the default workspace.

Choose Share
A message appears letting you know that the share process has started. As the share process runs, you'll receive a notification letting you know that the process started and a notification of when it finishes with the result. You'll also see that the Manage Access dialog continually refreshes until the share process finishes.

Databricks
Login to Databricks

Your data product is shared and its share status is updated and visible in the catalog search results. The name that you entered is the name that users will see in SAP Databricks, and it cannot be changed. If you need to change the name, you must delete the share setting for the data product and then share it to the target system again.

In conclusion, SAP Business Data Cloud provides a robust and scalable framework to transform raw data into business-ready insights. By leveraging Object Store for data ingestion, SAP Datasphere for modeling, and seamless data sharing to platforms like Databricks, organizations can unlock the full potential of their data. This approach not only ensures performance and governance but also enables cross-platform analytics in a consistent and efficient manner.

SAP Datasphere SAP Business Data Cloud SAP Databricks Object Store on SAP BTP

entry.title: SAP Enterprise Support Academy Newsletter April 2026
entry.content:
Dear customers,
Find here a preview of the SAP Enterprise Support Academy monthly newsletter on latest news, training courses, upcoming sessions, and further educational offerings.
The SAP Enterprise Support Academy provides expert guidance and high impact knowledge transfer services that enable you to innovate and be successful with your SAP solutions.

Subscribe to regularly receive latest news and stay informed about the wide range of educational offerings provided by the SAP Enterprise Support Academy that help you to build the digital skills tailored to your needs.

Ensuring a Smooth SAP Datasphere Deployment

Preparing to deploy SAP Datasphere as part of SAP Business Data Cloud? The continuous quality check (CQC) for deployment readiness helps identify configuration risks early and ensures that your setup aligns with SAP Best Practices before go-live.

Start today

SAP Customer Experience Value Packages

Connect common customer experience challenges with the capabilities available in SAP Customer Experience solutions. Learn how Value Packages help turn CX goals into practical improvements.
