entry.title: Hybrid Data Modeling in SAP BDC: a simple Framework for product-oriented architecture
entry.content:
Intro

When facing a transformation project towards SAP BDC, the real question should be where each piece of logic belongs in a product-oriented architecture: close to the source as a primary data product, or in Datasphere as a derived one.

That boundary depends on semantics, ownership, and the BW starting point — especially whether you are on BW/4HANA PCE or BW 7.5 PCE. This is why the unit of exchange matters.

In BDC, the architectural shift is from objects to data products, and that change has direct consequences for how modeling decisions should be made.
BW PCE and Datasphere are not competing layers. They serve different purposes, and the strongest architectures place each logic block where it creates the most semantic value, the least technical debt, and the clearest ownership.
For those who prefer an infographic, here is a visual summary:

Where You Start Matters

Not all BW landscapes enter BDC from the same position, and the hybrid strategy changes depending on the BW release you run.
If you are on BW/4HANA PCE, you are in the cleanest architectural position. Your objects are already BW/4HANA-compliant — ADSOs, Composite Providers, InfoObjects — and the Data Product Generator can work with them directly. From there, the main decision is which BW PCE objects should become certified primary data products and which logic should be rebuilt natively in Datasphere. BW/4HANA PCE is supported at least until 2040, so there is no need to rush the architecture.
If you are on BW 7.5 PCE, the situation is different. BW 7.5 mainstream maintenance ends in 2027, with extended support available until 2030 at a premium. That matters, but the bigger issue is the legacy object gap. Many traditional BW 7.5 objects — classic Info-Cubes, Multi-Providers, older 3.x objects — cannot be used in BW/4HANA PCE and therefore will not be available in BDC after 2030. A lift-and-shift into PCE without addressing these objects simply delays the problem.

For BW 7.5, a credible hybrid strategy starts with an object-level assessment:

  • Which objects are BW/4HANA-compatible and can become data products now.
  • Which objects need conversion before they can be used in BDC.
  • Which logic should be rebuilt directly in Datasphere.

Standard SAP extractors such as 2LIS_02_ITM, 0CO_OM_CCA_9, and 2LIS_03_BF also require careful treatment when moving from BW 7.5 to BDC. They need a deliberate integration strategy, not an assumption of clean portability.

The Conceptual Shift: Stop Thinking in Objects, Start Thinking in Products

The key mindset shift in BDC is simple: the architectural unit is no longer the object, but the data product.
In traditional BW, consumers reach into queries, InfoProviders, and extractors. In BDC, the focus moves to data products that carry metadata, ownership, lineage, and a consumer contract. That is a deeper change than a terminology update.
This changes the modeling question. The relevant question is no longer whether something should stay in BW or move to Datasphere. The better question is whether that logic belongs in a primary data product or a derived one, and who should own it.
BDC uses three product types that matter in practice: 

  • SAP-managed data products are primary products delivered by SAP from source applications such as S/4HANA Public Cloud or RISE.
  • Customer-managed data products are primary products you create in Datasphere from BW-originated or other data, using your own domain logic.
  • Derived data products are secondary, consumer-oriented datasets that combine and enrich multiple primary sources.

The practical implication is straightforward: BW PCE is a strong producer of primary data products, while Datasphere is the place to curate, combine, and distribute derived ones. The boundary between the two is a product contract, not a migration line.

AI-Readiness and Planning do not shift the paradigm

What about AI and Planning? they would deserve their own article, but bottom line they confirm the principle.
AI does not introduce a new modeling principle; it raises the quality bar.

The same boundary rules that govern reporting also govern AI: preserve semantic correctness and lineage in the primary product layer, then publish governed derived products for consumption by analytics, SAP-native AI services, SAP Databricks, or other platforms accessible via BDC Connect and zero-copy sharing.

The same applies to Planning, your planning processes can succeed only if their baseline data layer is consistent, reliable, curated. This of course is not enough to have outstanding budgeting and forecasting processes, but it's mandatory.
Those reinforce that overall framework it's not only a matter of layers or platforms, it's a matter of having a governed ecosystem able to produce data products that can capture the whole complexity and completeness of business semantics.

What BW Does Best

BW PCE remains the right place for logic that depends on SAP semantics and needs deep built-in handling.
Currency translation is a good example. Multi-step translation paths, exchange rate types, fiscal-year-aware conversion, and runtime target currency selection are native BW strengths. Datasphere can reproduce this, but only by rebuilding logic that BW already handles well.
Delta extraction is another example. For many SAP source systems, especially in BW 7.5 landscapes, extraction logic is not a trivial technical layer. It often contains business rules that should remain stable and governed. In many cases, the right approach is to preserve that extraction logic, then expose the result as a governed data product for Datasphere consumption.
Non-cumulative key figures are also a BW strength. Stock quantities, headcount, and open items depend on exception aggregation behaviors such as MIN, MAX, and LAST. Those patterns don't have yet a similar coverage by the Datasphere Analytical Model.
InfoObject-based master data governance is another area where BW still has the edge. Time-dependent attributes, compounding, and SID-based joins provide referential integrity that Datasphere’s more flexible dimension model does not automatically replicate.

What Datasphere Does Best

Datasphere is not a replacement for BW. It is the right place for breadth, composition, and domain ownership.
Its strongest use case is the creation of derived data products. A Finance product from BW, an HR product from SuccessFactors, and an external benchmark source can be combined in Datasphere into a single analytical model such as cost per FTE by region. BW was not designed for this kind of cross-domain composition.
Datasphere is also the right place for domain-driven ownership. Its space model supports a data mesh operating model, where Finance, Supply Chain, and HR can each own and govern their own derived products independently. That gives teams clear accountability and reduces dependency on a central modeling team.
Consumption-layer modernization belongs here as well. New analytical models should be built in Datasphere, not as additional BW queries. The gap with BW query semantics is still real, but building new consumption artifacts in BW PCE only adds more logic to a layer that will eventually need to be unwound.

SAP-managed data products also need to be part of the decision. If SAP already provides a product that covers the domain adequately, that is often the right starting point. In that case, neither a custom BW model nor a custom Datasphere model should be the default answer.

A Simple Decision Framework

A good hybrid design becomes much easier if you ask three questions for each piece of logic.

1. Does this logic produce a primary or derived product?

If the logic extracts governed SAP data, applies BW-specific semantics, manages currency conversion, or handles delta logic, it is producing a primary product. It should stay as close to the authoritative source as possible.
If the logic combines multiple primary products or enriches SAP data with non-SAP sources, it is producing a derived product. That belongs in Datasphere.

2. How many consumers depend on it?

A small experimental dashboard can move quickly. A reconciled P&L report or a statutory close process cannot. The more consumers depend on the logic, the more important it is to validate equivalence before switching over.
That is where the data product contract helps. You can publish both versions, validate them in parallel, and move consumers gradually instead of forcing a hard cutover.

3. What does the legacy object inventory look like?

This is the critical BW 7.5 question. Classic InfoCubes, MultiProviders, and 3.x-era objects cannot be used in BW/4HANA PCE and therefore have no path into BDC after 2030.
Before you design a hybrid architecture on BW 7.5, you need an object compatibility assessment. That assessment will tell you whether BW PCE can still serve as a short-term primary product layer for a given domain, or whether that domain should move to Datasphere-native modeling sooner.

Where Projects usually break

Naive migration usually fails in the same places.
BW query semantics do not survive simple translation. Exception aggregation, non-cumulative key figures, and variable-driven dynamic filters do not map cleanly to Datasphere Analytical Models. The result is often subtle numeric differences that only appear under specific hierarchy settings or filter combinations.
Delta capture is defined at the source and is not reversible downstream. The source ADSO determines what can be loaded incrementally into Datasphere, and that cannot be fixed later. If delta handling is wrong at the beginning, the downstream design will inherit that mistake.
The legacy object gap remains a major risk in BW 7.5 landscapes. If you lift and shift without addressing object compatibility, the issue simply reappears later at higher cost.
SAP-managed data products create another common trap. They remain immutable, and custom Z-tables are not currently supported in them. Any extension pattern involving custom fields or tables should therefore be designed as a customer-managed derived product from the start.
Authorization is another area that requires redesign, not configuration. BW analysis authorizations do not have a direct one-to-one equivalent in Datasphere Data Access Control, so security needs to be treated as its own workstream.

Closing the Loop

The strongest BDC architectures treat the boundary between BW and Datasphere as a product contract, not a migration deadline.
Some logic belongs close to the source because it carries SAP semantics that are difficult to reproduce elsewhere. Some logic belongs in Datasphere because it combines sources, serves a domain, and creates new analytical value. SAP-managed products should be used whenever they already cover the need. The job of the architect is to place each piece of logic where it creates the best semantic result.
That is the real shift in BDC: not faster migration, but better placement. Not object thinking, but product thinking. And once that shift is made, the architecture becomes much clearer, much more defensible, and much easier to evolve.

entry.title: Configuring Single Sign-On (SSO) for SAP Datasphere using SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS)
entry.content:
In today’s enterprise landscape, managing secure and seamless access across multiple applications is not just a convenience - it’s a necessity. As organizations expand their SAP ecosystem, implementing a centralized authentication mechanism becomes critical for both user experience and security.

In this blog, I will walk through in detail on how Single Sign-On (SSO) can be effectively implemented for SAP Datasphere using SAP Cloud Identity Services, specifically leveraging the SAP Identity Authentication Service, integrated with OKTA. Microsoft Active Directory integration follows similar procedure.

What is Single Sign-On (SSO)?

Single Sign-On (SSO) is an authentication mechanism that allows users to log in once and gain access to multiple applications without being prompted to log in again.

Instead of managing multiple credentials, users authenticate through a trusted identity provider, which securely handles access across integrated systems.

Key Objective:

  • One Identity
  • One Login
  • Multiple Secure Access Points

What is SAP Datasphere?

SAP Datasphere is SAP’s next-generation cloud-based data warehousing solution that enables organizations to unify, analyze, and manage data across landscapes.

It provides:

  • Seamless data integration from SAP and non-SAP sources
  • Advanced analytics and data modeling capabilities
  • Business-ready semantic layers

Given its critical role in enterprise data strategy, secure and efficient user access becomes essential.

What are SAP Cloud Identity Services?

SAP Cloud Identity Services is SAP’s central identity management platform designed to handle authentication, authorization, and identity lifecycle management across SAP and non-SAP applications.

It consists of two main components:

  • Identity Authentication Service (IAS)
  • Identity Provisioning Service (IPS)

This blog focuses on IAS as the authentication engine for SSO.

What is SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS)?

SAP Identity Authentication Service is a cloud-based identity provider that manages user authentication and provides secure access to SAP applications.

It supports:

  • SAML 2.0, OAuth, OpenID Connect
  • Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)
  • Conditional authentication policies

Architecture Overview: SSO with SAP IAS as Proxy

In this implementation:

  • IAS acts as a Proxy Identity Provider
  • Authentication requests are redirected to OKTA
  • IAS validates and passes authentication back to SAP Applications
  • Email address is used as the Unique User Identifier (Attribute)

Flow Simplified:

  1. User accesses SAP Datasphere
  2. Redirected to IAS (Proxy Identity Provider)
  3. IAS forwards authentication to OKTA
  4. User logs in via corporate credentials
  5. IAS validates and grants access
  6. User gains seamless access to SAP Datasphere

Why use Email as the User Attribute?

Using email as a unique identifier ensures:

  • Global uniqueness across systems
  • Alignment with corporate identity standards
  • Simplified user mapping across SAP and non-SAP applications

Benefits of Single Sign-On (SSO)

  1. Enhanced User Experience
  • Eliminates repeated logins
  • Faster access to applications
  • Reduced password fatigue

      2. Improved Security

  • Centralized authentication control
  • Enables Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)
  • Reduces attack surface from password reuse
  1. Increased Productivity
  • Less time spent on login issues
  • Streamlined access to enterprise tools

End-to-End Configuration Steps

SAP IAS – OKTA Integration

Step 1: Extract the IAS Tenant Metadata: Login IAS as Administrator -> Applications & Resources -> Tenant Settings -> Single Sign-On -> SAML 2.0 Configuration -> Download Metadata File

Step 2: Configure IAS Tenant in OKTA: Login OKTA Admin Portal as Administrator -> Click on the 'Use single sign on' - 'Add App' option -> Create New App -> Platform = Web | Sign on method = SAML 2.0 -> Create

Provide IAS Tenant Description -> Maintain Single Sign-On Endpoint URL from IAS (ACS End Point) as Single Sign-On URL |  Recipient URL | Destination URL. Audience Restriction -> IAS Domain. Name ID Format -> E-mail Address. Digest Algorithm -> SHA256 -> Save.

Navigate to the 'Sign On' tab -> Click on 'Identity Provider metadata' hyperlink -> Export Configuration Metadata into an XML file

Step 3: Configure OKTA as Trusted Corporate Identity Provider : Login IAS Portal as Administrator -> Click on Identity Providers -> Corporate Identity Providers -> Create

Go to SAML 2.0 Configuration -> Browse and upload the OKTA Metadata XML file -> Save

Step 4: SAP Datasphere - IAS Configuration:

Only a System Owner can perform Single Sign-On Administration activity in SAP Datasphere.

Assign the System Owner Privilege with valid email address

Navigate to Information -> Administration

Step 4.1: Go to Security Tab -> Click on Change icon -> Authentication Method -> Choose SAML Single Sign-On (SSO)

Step 4.2: Under SAML Single Sign-On (SSO) Configuration -> Download Service Provider Metadata -> Extract SAP Datasphere Tenant’s Metadata (Here SAP Datasphere is the Service Provider)

We will complete parallel configuration in IAS for SAP Datasphere and resume further steps for seamless verification process

Step 4.2.1: Configure SAP Datasphere Application in IAS

Open new browser session -> Login IAS Tenant as Administrator -> Applications & Resources -> Applications -> Create -> Provide Display Name -> Choose Type as SAP Datasphere Solution -> Protocol Type – SAML2.0

Go to SAML 2.0 Configuration -> Upload SAP Datasphere Metadata (Service Provider) extracted from Step 4.2

Maintain Subject Name Identifier with Email as Primary Attribute. It is optional to configure fallback attribute.

Default Name ID Format -> Email

By default, Conditional Authentication will be set with Identity Authentication as Default Identity Provider

Resuming Configuration in SAP Datasphere -> Administrator -> Security

 Step 4.3: Under SAML Single Sign-On (SSO) Configuration -> Upload Identity Provider Metadata -> Upload IAS Tenant Metadata extracted from Step 1

Step 4.4: Under Confirm that the mapping is working, provide the System Owner E-mail Address -> Verify Account -> Follow instructions as per prompt

Note: System Owner ID must exist in IAS with correct e-mail address maintained

Step 4.5: Click Save icon to enable SAML Single Sign-On -> Convert

Authentication Verification with IAS: Try login SAP Datasphere Tenant in an Incognito / Private Browser session. Authentication to happen by IAS.

 Enabling IAS as Proxy:

Go to Conditional Authentication of SAP Datasphere Application and change the Default Identity Provider to the OKTA Corporate Identity Provider created in Step 3.

Authentication Verification with IAS as Proxy : Try login SAP Datasphere Tenant in an Incognito / Private Browser session. Authentication to happen by OKTA via IAS.

Conclusion

Implementing SSO for SAP Datasphere using SAP Cloud Identity Services - SAP Identity Authentication Service provides a robust, scalable, and secure authentication framework.

By configuring IAS as Proxy for Corporate Identity Provider, organizations can achieve a unified authentication strategy that enhances both user experience and security posture.

Author’s Note

This implementation reflects a real-world enterprise approach to consolidating authentication mechanisms across SAP landscapes using modern identity services.

If you're planning your Single Sign-On (SSO) journey or optimizing SAP Applications Architecture, this model serves as a strong foundation.

Referral Links

  • https://me.sap.com/notes/0002943651
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/connect-okta-to-identity-authentication/ba-p/13468665

#SAP #SingleSignOn #SSO #SAPDatasphere #SAPCloudIdentityServices #SAPIdentityAuthenticationService #SAPIAS #OKTA

entry.title: SAP BW Bridge or Data Product Generator (DPG): What would you choose
entry.content:
Ankit Sarkar 

  • Certified SAP Datasphere and Databricks Consultant
  • RG Technologies (2+ years of experience)

 

SAP BW Bridge and Data Product Generator (DPG)

Introduction

A common question arises - Should you expose your data for modern consumption, or should you migrate your BW system?

Organisations are moving from traditional on-premise BW systems to the cloud. So, the above question commonly arises.

The solution is BW Bridge and Data Product Generator (DPG). SAP offers two powerful options to migrate your BW system. You may see that they seem similar at first, but there are some huge differences between how they serve for your transformation journey.

SAP BW Bridge - Lift Your System to the Cloud: 

BW Bridge provides a way to migrate your existing BW system to the cloud with minimal disruption.

  • You can reuse the objects like DSOs, Info-Objects, and Transformations from the existing BW system.
  • You can entertain ABAP-based extraction logics.
  • You can keep on using classic extractors from SAP ECC and SAP S/4HANA.
  • Within SAP Datasphere, you can operate in a dedicated ABAP-enabled environment.

Use Cases : 

  • Companies use BW Bridge to move on-premise SAP/4HANA or SAP BW 7.x systems to SAP Datasphere without rebuilding data structures from scratch.
  • Organisations that excessively count on complex SAP S/API or ODP extractors, so with common methods, you can't connect directly to the cloud. Then you need the BW Bridge to connect with the cloud.
  • BW Bridge offers a staging area for complex transformations and cleaning before making the data available in Datasphere.

 SAP Data Product Generator (DPG) : 

You can use a different approach with DPG, instead of moving the whole system, DPG focuses on preparing your BW data usable in modern ecosystems like SAP Datasphere and Databricks.

  • You can replicate Info-Providers.
  • In tools like SAP Analytics Cloud or platforms like Databricks, you can do zero-copy consumption.
  • With the process chain, you can apply filters and schedule loads.
  • You can carry through full and delta data extraction.

Use Cases : 

  • When you need to migrate an on-premise BW system to a cloud system, you also need to keep the existing logics.
  • When you need to enable BW Info-providers in SAP Datasphere.
  • When you need to quickly bring out data models in Datasphere.
  • You need to perform AI/ML modelling with an external platform like Databricks, then you can share trusted, structured BW data with it.

 

Differences Between SAP BW Bridge and Data Product Generator (DPG) : 

AspectsSAP BW BridgeData Product Generator (DPG)
Core PurposeBW systems migrationReusable data products creation
Main FocusData Processing & Data WarehousingData Consumption & Data Sharing
Architecture RoleData Transformation and Data StorageData Reuse and Data Exposure
LayerModelling Layer or BackendBusiness Layer or Consumption
Data TransformationCan use data Transformation (ETL Logic)Cannot use Data Transformation
Data ModellingSupports full BW ModellingModelling is not used in DPG
Data Product CreationBW Bridge does not create any Data ProductData Product Creation is the core functionality of DPG
Data Storage It can store and manage dataIt uses the existing data
FlexibilityLess Flexible (BW Structure)More Flexible (Modern Approach)
ReusabilityLessHigh
Use CaseBW to Datasphere MigrationCurated Datasets sharing
Cloud Native To some extentCompletely 
Modern RelevanceLagacy MigrationFuture ready
Target UsersBW Developer or Data EngineersData Consumers or Business Users

Conclusion 

If you are shifting to SAP Datasphere, this is a common confusion about which service you need to use. 

In this Blog, I have explained some differences, use cases, and some practical examples or ways so you can decide which service you would use, whether you want to migrate your BW system or explore your data for a modern approach.

entry.title: Sizing the SAP Datasphere Object Store
entry.content:
What Do We Need To Size

The  SAP Datasphere Object Store consists of three features:

  1. The object store to hold data, that includes the data in the tables, metadata  logs, and back-up snapshots.
  2. The Apache Spark engine as the compute for all operations, like creating delta tables, transformations, and housekeeping.
  3. SAP HANA Native SQL on Files the SQL on files engine in SAP HANA, as an bridge between the object store and the relational world of SQL, that is used when reporting on data in the object store, when previewing it, when querying it in any fashion and more.

The SAP Datasphere Object Store meters three distinct, but dependent measures.

  • Object Store Storage - is the sum of all files and folders stored in the object store of an SAP Datasphere space.
  • Object Store Compute - is the total amount of 4 GB Memory hours (which combined CPU, Memory and temporary disk storage) used by all Spark jobs.
  • Object Store Requests - occur during all interactions with the object store, e.g. reading a file (get OPEN), writing a file (put CREATE), deleting a file (del DELETE) and many more (see documentation).

Data Volumes and Compression

We must be aware that the same records stored in a raw format, local tables (HANA) SAP HANA, or local tables (file) (stored as delta tables in SAP HANA Data Lake Files) use different amounts of storage. Compression rates can vary based on the actual data and its data types. One assumption that can be used is:

1 GB CSV (Raw) -> ~1/8 GB in HANA
1 GB HANA  -> 1.35 GB in delta tables

For the performance calculation, we will use the SAP HANA Data Lake Files (HDLF) data volume.

Object Store Storage

The total data footprint consists of the initially loaded data plus the incremental delta loads accumulated over time.

When data is loaded into a local table (file) using a Replication Flow, it is first written to an inbound buffer. Then a merge task has to be completed so that the data is moved into the underlying delta table.

Data files in delta tables are immutable. Any update, delete, or merge operation generates new files and record ‘remove file’ actions for the old ones. As a result, overwriting data increases storage usage because previous file versions must be retained. These older files are needed for delta extraction and must remain available until all delta subscribers have consumed their data. They must be removed later by a vacuum job. The amount of storages used by these old versions depends on whether the source provides change data (updates/deletes) or only new inserts

The object store storage can be calculated by combining the initially loaded data and adding the periodic load volume that is added per load, times the frequency of the periodic load and the version retention period. In the worst-case scenario, all files receive an update during a delta load, which would cause the table to be re-written with a slightly larger size. 

Here, SAP is working on multiple improvements to help minimize this growth, see the Roadmap Item 

The best-case scenario is when only inserts are delivered and files are never changed, in which case the table storage only grows by its added volume of data during a merge.

The object store keeps a backup of all deleted files for disaster recovery over a period of 14 days.

The versions and backup should level out eventually, when the retention periods are reached and a new version comes in, as an old version leaves the system.

Storage is metered hourly. That results in the fact that the monthly CU charged is not the size at the end of the month time hours in the month, it is rather the integral over time. For a linear graph, it’s the the average data size multiplied by the number of hours in the month (for estimations we assume 730h per month). Or in other words we need to estimate the average active data size in our system.

Object Store Compute

Object Store Compute is measured in blocks, based on 4 GB/h. This is because all Apache Spark configurations currently have a 1 CPU to 4 GB memory ratio.

Therefore, we need to estimate how many hours Apache Spark will run. This is the sum of hours spent on:

  • Task runs in the object store (spaces with storage type "SAP HANA Data Lake Files")
  • Deployments in the object store (spaces with storage type "SAP HANA Data Lake Files")
  • System Tasks
    • Statistic Updates 
    • Housekeeping jobs (like cleanup of logs)

For merge and transformation flow tasks, the required object store compute depends on many factors:

Data Characteristics

  • Volume - total data size drives memory and parallelism needs
  • Cardinality - high-cardinality columns (e.g. user IDs) affect shuffle size in groupBy/join
  • Skew - uneven key distribution causes some tasks to process far more data than others
  • Schema width - wide rows (many columns) increase serialization overhead
  • Write pattern - insert-only workloads produce fewer redundant files than upsert/delete-heavy ones, so Optimize is cheaper after them.

Transformation Type

  • Narrow vs. wide transformations - filter, map, select stay within a partition; groupBy, join, distinct, repartition cause shuffles (expensive)
  • Shuffle operations - triggered by wide transformations; move data across the network and write intermediate results to disk
  • User defined functions (UDF) - python UDFs especially are slow (JVM ↔ Python serialization); prefer built-in Spark SQL functions

Workload Configuration

  • Executor memory/cores - determine parallelism and whether spill to disk occurs
  • Dynamic allocation - lets Spark scale executors based on workload

For optimize, the required object store compute depends on:

  • Number of small files - the main cost driver. Each write operation (merge, load, upsert) appends new files and creates new versions. The more fragmented the table, the more spark workload optimize must use to compact them. This makes optimize costs grow with write frequency, not just data size.
  • Table size (total data volume) - larger tables take longer to compact even at the same file count.
  • Z-ORDER BY - if column co-location (Z-ordering) is applied, Optimize must physically reshuffle data across files to group related rows. This is significantly more compute-intensive than simple compaction.

For an overview on best practices on optimizing consumption of the object store see the SAP Note 3722983

Object Store Requests

Object store requests are created during any read and write interaction with the object store. Besides the Spark-based tasks we need to add the object store requests from the Replication Flow writing into the inbound buffer table and we need to consider data access by SQL on File (SoF) calls.

Object store requests will be the combination of:

  • Task runs in the object store (spaces with storage type "SAP HANA Data Lake Files")
  • Replication Flow writes into Inbound Buffer
  • Deployments in the object store (spaces with storage type "SAP HANA Data Lake Files")
  • SQL on Files (e.g. cross-space sharing to HANA spaces or Data Preview)

Factors that impact object store requests:

  • Access method to local tables (file) (delta/active) - delta access requires additional access to logs and versions, while the volume in delta is typically smaller

Merge

  • Data volume in Inbound Buffer - determines how many target files are candidates for rewrite
  • Insert-only vs update/delete - insert-only merge can append new files; updates/deletes require rewriting matched files. When only inserts occur, it skips expensive target file rewrite

SQL on File reads into HANA

  • Total number of files in local table (file) -  fewer large files vs. many small files (influenced by frequency of merge or optimize runs)
  • Partition pruning - filters on partition columns eliminate entire directories → fewer calls
  • Column pruning - only requested columns are fetched (fewer bytes, same file count)

HANA can be used to cache data for reads, especially for SAP Analytics Cloud. Related best practice is to create data marts in spaces with SAP HANA Cloud storage (local tables (HANA) or persisted views) for reporting scenarios with frequent use and SLAs requiring high-performance.  There are ways to mitigate the full reads in SAP Datasphere persisted views . You can partition views and lock partitions that don’t change, preventing unnecessary data read. To enable this, ensure that the partitions defined in the view are aligned with the partitions defined in the Local Table (File),  so that partition locking effectively avoids reading the underlying files.

Another alternative is to use local tables (HANA) instead of view persistency.  This allows the system to load only the deltas from the Local Tables (File), so it reads only the changes. As a result, the number of object store requests is significantly reduced.

SQL on Files object store requests run by queries (such as MDS queries, previews, etc.) are even more difficult to estimate, as there is typically no available data to base these calculations on. 

For an overview on best practices on optimizing consumption of the object store see the SAP Note 3722983

Input for sizing

  • Find your average active data size
  • Estimate how many small (<5GB), medium (5GB-50GB) and large (> 50GB) tables you have and what percentage of tables have changes, and which are insert-only
  • Define data availability and update frequency for tables based on end user requirements and object store consumption. More frequent merge runs increase object store consumption.
  • Determine the number of potential re-initializations per year, for example required for incompatible schema changes in connected sources
  • Make an assumption about data retention (housekeeping on data performed with delete and subsequent vacuum processes).

If you need support to come up with an estimate, reach out to your SAP contact (Sales, Pre-Sales, COE)

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) Onboarding
entry.content:
Prerequisites

  1. The BDC license is purchased, and you have received the welcome email. After your organization has purchased SAP Business Data Cloud, the user designated as the contact person receives an invitation email with a link to SAP for Me. If you’re unsure about your registration as a contact person for SAP Business Data Cloud, contact your Sales Account Executive or the Customer Interaction Center (CIC).

    If you are from a third party of the SAP customer, then reach out to your contact on the SAP customer side. They must provide you with this email. Note: Do not open a support Case with SAP Support as they cannot resend or provide you with this email.

  2. You will also require an S-user with Super Admin or Cloud Admin access in SAP for Me. Refer to KBA 2492132 What is the difference between Super, Cloud, and User Administrator S-user IDs? - SAP for Me and more information available here Understanding S-Users and SAP for Me

Outline Steps

  1. Provisioning the BDC Cockpit is the first step and you cannot proceed to provision anything else until this step is completed. We provide step by step instructions in the Community Blog Part 1: Provisioning of Business Data Cloud through SAP4Me
  2. Then provision the BDC Core components of Datasphere, SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC), Databricks as per your business requirements. Refer to steps in Provisioning SAP Business Data Cloud Applications

Resources

Before embarking on the provisioning process, it is important to educate yourself on the entire process.

  1. The Business Data Cloud Resource Center is your starting point for this journey



    There are many resources available here including: 
    1. the SAP BDC Onboarding Webcast which should be mandatory viewing for anyone who is about to provision SAP BDC.
    2. full video of the provisioning process here: Provisioning of SAP Business Data Cloud and its core components
    3. SAP BDC Onboarding – this is the official product documentation which explains in detail the entire provisioning process.
    4. SAP BDC Community Blog series here Introductory Guide to Provisioning of SAP Business Data Cloud – this has a series of blogs with screenshots showing the step by step process.

  2. If you still have questions or if you would like to have an SAP onboarding expert walk you through the provisioning process, you can book a 1:1 onboarding session with SAP via SAP Business Data Cloud -> “If you still have questions, request a 1:1 onboarding session here”.

  3. If you encounter technical issues during the actual provisioning process, then you may open a Support Case via the SAP for Me portal.
  4. If you have questions outside of SAP Technical Support area of responsibility or if you would like to leverage the knowledge and experience of other SAP BDC Administrators and users, post your questions to the SAP Business Data Cloud - SAP Community Q&A space.

Additional Resources

  • Official Product documentation here: SAP Business Data Cloud
  • SAP Community Blog SAP Business Data Cloud - From Provisioning to Intelligent Applications
  • SAP Community Blog SAP Business Data Cloud - FAQs
  • SAP Community Blog Rewiring of SAP Datasphere to SAP Business Data Cloud
  • KBA 3733691 SAP BDC Onboarding FAQ
  • KBA 2626344 Who is the SAP Account Manager/Executive for my company?
  • KBA 3705747 Provisioning failure due to duplicate host name during SAP Business Data Cloud Cockpit
